title: The Life of a Star
content:
On a clear night far from city lights, you can see thousands of stars scattered across the sky. Each one is a sun, a vast blazing ball of fire millions of miles across, burning brightly in the darkness of space. Stars look small and still from here, but they live long, dramatic lives.

Stars are born inside enormous clouds of gas and dust called nebulae. A nebula can be so huge that it takes light thousands of years to cross it. Right now, deep inside nebulae throughout our galaxy, new stars are being born.

Gravity slowly pulls the gas and dust together into a giant spinning ball that gets hotter and hotter as it shrinks. When the center reaches millions of degrees, hydrogen atoms begin to fuse together, releasing a tremendous burst of energy. At that moment, a star is born.

Our own sun is a medium-sized star. It was born about 4.6 billion years ago. It will continue to shine for another five billion years. In the universe, our sun is quite ordinary, but to every living thing on Earth, it is the most important star of all.

A star's lifetime depends on how much hydrogen fuel it has. Massive stars burn their fuel very quickly and may explode after only a few million years. Smaller stars burn slowly and can shine for trillions of years. Our sun will live for about ten billion years in total.

When a star like our sun begins to run low on fuel, it swells up into a giant red ball called a red giant. Eventually it puffs off its outer layers in a glowing shell of gas, leaving behind a small, dense, slowly cooling core called a white dwarf.

Very massive stars end their lives far more dramatically. They explode in a colossal blast called a supernova, shining briefly brighter than a billion suns. The explosion flings all the elements the star created during its life, including carbon, oxygen, iron, and gold, out across space.

Here is the most astonishing thing of all: the atoms in your body were once inside a star. The iron in your blood was forged in a stellar furnace. The calcium in your bones was scattered by a supernova billions of years ago. When you look up at the night sky, you are looking at your own ancient origins. We are, as scientists say, made of star stuff.

author: Educational
